tests.title: Observation Angle Follow-up (score=0, turn 1)
tests.implemented: Dynamic angle-aware prompt injects observation angle suggestion into follow-up question generator.
tests.scenario: Object: apple | Attribute: color | Age: 5 | Turn 1, interest_score=0, observation angle

params.response_text: Apples can be red, green, or yellow!
params.attribute_soft_guide:
SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
- Default to sight: 'What do you notice?', 'Which part looks the biggest?', 'Is it shiny or dull?'
- Allow imagination/guessing: 'Do you think it would feel rough?', 'If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?'
- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object
- Do NOT use 'Do you know…' framing
- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: color
Turns explored: 1
Angles already used: (none yet)

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Current attribute: color
Current interest score: 0/100
Session turns: 1
Explored attributes: appearance.color

HANDOFF MODE: INACTIVE
Continue exploring the current attribute. Do NOT output [ACTIVITY_READY].

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: observation]
Ask the child to observe and describe the attribute with their own words

For this turn, try using the observation angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Share one concrete sensory fact about the color
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask what the child notices or sees about the color
- Example of a good question: 'What color do you see on the apple?'

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
(none yet)

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- 'What color is it?' -- quiz
- 'Do you know what color it has?' -- quiz with wrapper
- 'What else can you tell me about it?' -- too vague
- 'Let us look at its color!' -- forced redirect
- 'That is nice, but...' then question about color -- ignoring child
- 'Great! Now we can start an activity!' -- mechanical announcement
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY] after just one shallow exchange -- premature handoff
- Switching topics on a single casual mention -- too sensitive
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Does NOT use 'Do you know' framing

tests.title: Comparison Angle Follow-up (score=35, turn 2)
tests.implemented: After observation angle used, prompt suggests comparison angle to avoid repetition.
tests.scenario: Object: apple | Attribute: color | Age: 5 | Turn 2, interest_score=35, comparison angle

params.response_text: The red on this apple is super bright — almost like a little lantern!
params.attribute_soft_guide:
SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
- Default to sight: 'What do you notice?', 'Which part looks the biggest?', 'Is it shiny or dull?'
- Allow imagination/guessing: 'Do you think it would feel rough?', 'If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?'
- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object
- Do NOT use 'Do you know…' framing
- For imitation: only voices and stretches/movements are OK

HANDOFF MODE: INACTIVE
Continue exploring the current attribute. Do NOT output [ACTIVITY_READY].

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: comparison]
Compare this attribute with something familiar to the child

For this turn, try using the comparison angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Share a surprising comparison or contrast about the color
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask the child to compare the color with something they know
- Example of a good question: 'Is it more like a banana or a grape in color?'

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
- observation: What color do you see on the apple?

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- 'What color is it?' -- quiz
- 'Do you know what color it has?' -- quiz with wrapper
- 'What else can you tell me about it?' -- too vague
- 'Let us look at its color!' -- forced redirect
- 'That is nice, but...' then question about color -- ignoring child
- 'Great! Now we can start an activity!' -- mechanical announcement
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY] after just one shallow exchange -- premature handoff
- Switching topics on a single casual mention -- too sensitive
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Does NOT repeat 'What color do you see'

tests.title: HANDOFF MODE ACTIVE — Activity Bridge (score=68)
tests.implemented: When interest score >= 60, prompt tells model HANDOFF MODE: ACTIVE with activity name, expecting [ACTIVITY_READY] output.
tests.scenario: Object: apple | Attribute: color | Age: 5 | Turn 5, interest_score=68, should handoff to Color Matching Game

params.response_text: You know why apples turn red? Because they make a special red paint inside!
params.attribute_soft_guide:
SENSORY SAFETY (applies to all sensory invitations):
- Default to sight: 'What do you notice?', 'Which part looks the biggest?', 'Is it shiny or dull?'
- Allow imagination/guessing: 'Do you think it would feel rough?', 'If it rolled, would it go fast or slow?'
- Do NOT invite the child to TOUCH, SMELL, TASTE, LICK, or PHYSICALLY INTERACT with the object
- Do NOT use 'Do you know…' framing

[CONVERSATION COVERAGE]
Attribute: color
Turns explored: 5
Angles already used: observation, comparison, preference, association

---

[SYSTEM CONTEXT]
Current attribute: color
Current interest score: 68/100
Session turns: 5
Explored attributes: appearance.color

HANDOFF MODE: ACTIVE
Target attribute: appearance.color
Activity: Color Matching Game
Child interest score for this attribute: 68/100

Your next message should:
1. Naturally bridge from the current conversation to the activity
2. Introduce the activity by name
3. End with [ACTIVITY_READY]

---

[NEXT SUGGESTED ANGLE: causal]
Explore why or how the attribute came to be (age-appropriate)

For this turn, try using the causal angle:
- Your RESPONSE should: Give a simple, concrete explanation
- Your FOLLOW-UP QUESTION should: Ask why the apple has this color
- Example of a good question: 'Why do you think apples turn red when they grow?'

Already-used angles (try something different if possible):
- observation: What color do you see on the apple?
- comparison: Is it more like a banana or a grape in color?
- preference: Do you like red apples or green apples better?
- association: What else around you has this same color?

ANTI-PATTERNS -- NEVER produce these:
- 'What color is it?' -- quiz
- 'Do you know what color it has?' -- quiz with wrapper
- 'What else can you tell me about it?' -- too vague
- 'Let us look at its color!' -- forced redirect
- 'That is nice, but...' then question about color -- ignoring child
- 'Great! Now we can start an activity!' -- mechanical announcement
- Adding [ACTIVITY_READY] after just one shallow exchange -- premature handoff
- Switching topics on a single casual mention -- too sensitive
- Re-phrasing a question from an already-used angle

checks.criterion: Mentions activity name (Color Matching or similar)
